我们认识了很长时间，对共同的价值观、历史和生态系统有默契，也很清楚我们想做什么。我们之间有深层的互相尊重和互补的技能组合，合作得非常好。我非常感恩。任何创业经历都很难，尤其是像这样高强度的创业，如果身边没有一个你深度信任和连接的联合创始人，真的非常艰难。我见过有人独自做到了，但确实很难。所以我非常感激能和 Greg 一起走过这段路。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** Obviously, you and John knew each other for longer than Greg and I did. Um, but Greg and I did know each other for a long time before OpenAI and I think having the shared history really helps. One of the things that I had observed at Y Combinator was that one of the biggest predictors of success was had the co-founders known each other for a long time or at least relative to their lives for a long time and the teams that came together like you know 7 days before applying to YC on a co-founder matching site or whatever — that was that didn't work too often. It's not impossible. I think there are one or two cases where it did work but but it's rare.

Uh so we had known each other for a while and we had kind of had a sense of sort of shared values and history and ecosystem and kind of we were clear on what we wanted to do. Um and and I think we had this like deep mutual respect and complementary skill set that has just worked really well. Um the I'm extremely grateful. I think having to go through any startup experience, but particularly like an intense one without a co-founder you have like a deep connection trust to is is really hard. I've watched people do it, but it's very hard. So, um I am, you know, extremely grateful that we've gotten to do this together.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 换一个相关的话题。我们聊了 OpenAI，但还有整个围绕它的生态系统——各种公司、创业公司、企业都在你们的平台上构建产品。这对创业来说显然是一个有趣的时刻。一方面，构建产品和创造收入的速度似乎达到了前所未有的水平。我们在 Stripe 的数据中确实看到了这一点——企业达到重要营收阈值的速度比以往任何时候都快。你是有史以来最多产、最成功的创业投资人之一，也管理过 Y Combinator。在这个时代，让创始人成功的特质变了吗？还是跟以前一样？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** On an adjacent topic um you know we've talked about OpenAI but then there's this entire ecosystem um of companies and startups and uh and enterprises that are you know building on the platform. It's obviously an interesting moment in startups um given on the one hand the ability to build products and generate revenue at seemingly unprecedented rates and certainly we see this in the Stripe data like the number of businesses reaching thresholds um you know meaningful thresholds is far faster than it ever has been before. Um you are one of the most prolific and successful startup investors ever. You of course ran Y Combinator. Have the traits that make founders successful changed in this era or is it sort of the same thing it's always been?

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 以前我们常常嘲笑那种"点子人"。这些人想创业，说"我有一个最好的点子，我不告诉你是什么，但我有一个最好的点子。我只需要找个程序员帮我把它做出来，然后就万事大吉了。"我们会嘲笑这些人，他们确实也不太成功。对我来说这种心态一直让人恼火，因为这就像说"我有一首很棒的歌的想法，我只需要那个弹吉他的人帮我做出来就行了。"YC 也有一个版本的总结：没有技术联合创始人的团队很难成功。但突然之间，风水轮流转了——"点子人"的复仇来了。这对世界来说其实是好事，我很高兴能见证这一切。

但很长一段时间里，我、YC、我们这个行业在创始团队中最看重的素质是技术能力。这仍然很重要，但现在那些真正深刻理解用户、完全不会写代码的人——我想投资这些人了。这是一个很大的转变。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** Uh there was a time when we used to make fun of the idea guy. There were these people that wanted to start a company and they'd say like I have the best idea. I'm not going to tell you what it is. I have the best idea. I just need a coder to like build it for me and then I'm going to be in great shape. And we would like make fun of these people. They weren't that successful. Um the and it was like kind of always personally annoying to me because it's like it it would be like saying like I have a great idea for a song and I just need that guy with the guitar to like make it for me. Um and and so it kind of it didn't it didn't work like you know — YC had a version of this which is teams without technical founders are difficult to get work. All of a sudden it's like the revenge of the idea guys which is actually awesome for the world. Like I'm happy I'm here for it for sure.

Um but for a long time I think the most important ingredient that I looked for, YC looked for, that kind of this part of our industry looked for on a founding team was technical talent and that's still very important but now people who just really deeply understand their users and can't code at all I like want to fund those people and that's a that's a big turnaround.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 现在怎么思考创业投资？一方面，可能还有几年就到 AGI、ASI 或奇点了，谁知道呢。另一方面，基金的投资周期是 10 年。这两者怎么协调？能协调吗？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** How does one think about startup investing these days? Because on the one hand, you have a couple of years potentially to AGI or ASI or the Singularity, who knows what. And then you have investing time horizons or funds with 10-year time horizons. How does that all fit together? Does it?

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 我觉得在当下以 10 年为周期规划任何事情，都需要很大程度的"暂停怀疑"。但这可能是正确的生活方式。你不能因为觉得 3 年或 5 年后就是奇点、看不到未来，就什么都不做、放弃或者发疯。你必须按照"世界会以可理解的方式继续运转很长时间"来生活。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Uh I think to do anything at this point on a 10-year time horizon requires a real suspension of disbelief. Uh and yet that's probably the right way to live your life. Like I don't think it works to say there's this singularity in 3 years or 5 years, whatever. We can't see past it and so we're going to do nothing or we're just going to like give up or we're going to go crazy or whatever. Like you have to live as if stuff's just going to keep going in an understandable way for a long time.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** OpenAI 往前规划多远？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** How how far ahead does OpenAI plan?

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 我们签了 20 年的电力和土地协议。产品方面，我觉得我们对两年后的样子有比较清晰的愿景，再往后就模糊多了。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Uh I mean we signed like 20-year power and land agreements and for the product um I think we have like a clear vision of what things can look like in two years and then it gets much hazier after that.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 在不久之前有一种说法，说 GPT wrapper（GPT 包装器）和类似公司缺乏差异化、很脆弱，会被模型能力提升的浪潮冲走。但现在这种叙事似乎在反转——不再说"wrapper"了，而是说 harness（编排层），harness 被认为有显著的分量和价值。你怎么看这个问题？你怎么看那些以 AI 为关键驱动力的公司，它们的持久性如何？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** So there was in the relatively recent past uh a narrative that um you know GPT wrappers and companies of that ilk were um were I guess as the pejorative suggests undifferentiated and flimsy and be swept away by a rising tide of model improvements. Whereas now it feels like that narrative in some sense is flipping somewhat where now instead of talking about wrappers we talk about harnesses and harnesses are seen as having this you know significant heft and uh and importance and I guess I'm curious for your view on this and how you view uh businesses for which AI is a critical enabling component and their prospect of durability.

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 我从头到尾的观点一直是一样的：作为一家公司，你应该站在"希望 AI 变得更聪明"的那一边。在早期模型时代，如果你是个 GPT wrapper，做的事情本质上是修补当前模型的某个弱点，而这个弱点下一代模型显然会解决——那下一代模型更强的时候，你就难受了。但如果你做的东西随着智能提升会变得更好——比如人们用模型构建的各种精彩服务——那你会更开心。

Harness 时代也是同样的道理。我觉得正确的思维方式是：数据中心、模型、harness——整个系统是一个集群，输出的是高度可用的智能。有太多值得去构建的东西，你只会希望这个集群越来越好。而如果你暗自希望它不要变好，因为你在修补它的某个弱点——那么在这个技术栈的某个层面，下一次模型迭代可能就会直接把你的问题解决了。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** I I have kind of had uh the same view all the way through which is you as a business want to be on the side of hoping that AI gets smarter. Um and whether — so so like in the early model days if you were the GPT wrapper um and you were like patching some kind of weakness in the current model that was clearly going to get better with the next model. If the next model was much better you were kind of sad. If you were doing something that got better like you know you're making any of the wonderful like services that people were building with the models that benefited from intelligence you you would be happier.

I think the same thing in the world of harnesses like I I kind of think the right way to think about this is like data center model harness like that whole thing is just this one cluster out of which comes this like very usable intelligence. Um but there are so many things to go build where you're just like happier for that whole cluster to get better and better and better. Um, and then if you're kind of secretly hoping it doesn't because you're patching some weakness in that, probably like the next model crank turn somewhere in that stack is just going to solve it.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 当你观察那些对 AI 利用最有效的组织——你经常见 OpenAI 的客户，大公司和小公司都有——如果你想想印象最深的三家或者最深的一家，他们具体做了什么不同的事？在座每个人都知道 AI 是个大事，应该积极使用。但具体是什么让那些你认为用得最好的公司与众不同？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** When you look at the organizations that are making the most effective use of AI today, like if you um I mean you meet OpenAI customers constantly, uh, large and small, if you think about the top three that have impressed you the most or the one that's impressed you the most, what specifically are they doing that's different? Like everyone here knows yes AI big deal we should make enthusiastic use of it etc. But what specifically differentiates those which in your opinion are employing it most effectively?

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 几个不同的方向。我们共同的朋友 Toby Lütke，Shopify 的创始人，是我认识的第一个说"我们要在 AI 上全力以赴"的 CEO。他亲自动手，用 AI 自动化了所有事情，并且让团队也这么做。

他说我们要搞清楚怎么用 AI 把所有不好的事情变好。这不是什么 token 排行榜，也不是什么游戏化的可作弊的东西。就是公司 CEO 说了"我们现在要把 AI 融入我们做的每一件事，如果你不这么做，我不会满意。"这种能量后来也被其他人复制了。当公司 CEO 说"我们要把自己自动化、加速起来"，然后真的去执行，最好自己带头做——这种方式非常有效。

我们准备尝试一个新实验：派一个 FDE（现场开发工程师）直接跟公司 CEO 合作，帮他们自动化他们的工作，尽可能多地自动化。我觉得如果你只帮公司领导者做到了这一点，就会在整个公司产生一种漂亮的分形效应。这种方式是有效的，我们会帮公司做到这一点。

第二点是对数据访问权限保持"令人不舒服的开放态度"。有很多理由不这么做，我不是在推荐这种做法——你问的是我从最有效的公司那里看到了什么。这对小型创业公司比较容易，对有大量敏感数据和合规流程的公司比较难。但那些说"我们就是要录制会议、让 AI 访问代码库、让它看所有 Slack 消息、所有邮件、所有东西，而且每个员工都能这样使用"的公司——看着这些只有两三个人的创业公司加上 AI 做所有事情，真的令人震惊。数据隐私和 AI 效率之间的权衡，我不知道世界会怎么决定。有些监管可能需要调整。但这种方式真的极其强大。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** Uh a a few different directions there. Um friend of both of ours, uh Toby Lütke of Shopify was the first CEO I knew that just said like we are going to be all in on AI and the way we run our company and he got himself got his hands dirty just building like AI automation of everything and made his team do it.

Um and you know said we're just going to figure out how we take all these things that are bad and make them good with AI. And it was not like you know a token leaderboard. It was not some other kind of like gamified hackable thing. It was just like the CEO of the company said, "We are now going to put AI into everything we do and I'm going to like not be happy with you, I guess, or we're not, you know, we're not going to allow it if you're not doing that." So, that energy has now been done by other people. But when um when the CEO of a company just says like we're going to automate ourselves, accelerate ourselves, however they phrase it, internally um and then really holds people to it and ideally does it themselves. Um that has worked very well.

Uh, I think we're going to try this new experiment where we start sending like an FDE to work with like hands-on just whatever the CEO of a company needs work with them to automate their job. Um, as much of it as they can and that I think will have like if you just do it for the leader of a company, there's like a nice fractal effect throughout the company. So that works and we'll try to help companies do that.

Um, a second thing is being like uncomfortably permissive with data access. Um, there's huge reasons not to do this and I'm not — this is like I'm stopping short of recommendation. You just ask what I've seen from the most effective companies. Um this is easier for small startups than companies that have a lot of sensitive data and a lot of um you know like process and compliance in place but saying like you know what we are going to record our meetings we are going to like let this AI have access to our codebase we are going to let this have access to every Slack message every email everything and every employee at the company is going to get to use it that way — like it is amazing watching these two or three startups and AI doing everything work. Um, and I don't know how the world is going to decide the trade-offs on data privacy versus AI efficiency. And I think there's like some regulation that's going to have to change for that. Uh, but it's so powerful.

bilingual.zh:
**Patrick Collison：** Tempo 是 Stripe 和 Paradigm 一起孵化的一条新区块链——当然我们跟你们是合作伙伴。主网刚刚上线，但这个项目去年夏天就启动了，所以团队还比较新、比较小，大概二十几个人。Tempo 团队在 Slack 里搭建了一个编排工具，用来处理公司里几乎所有的事务。所有事情。你可以提出任何任务：去读这些 Google Docs，把它们转化成一系列 Linear 任务，然后去写一个 pull request 来实现它们，然后部署，再用日志分析工具测试部署是否成功——agent 会愉快地在所有这些工具之间使用 tool use 来完成。看着一个组织——虽然是小组织——在一个 Slack 频道里完成所有事情，非常超现实。

我不认为这种方式能直接扩展到 Stripe 的规模，但这是我第一次亲身体验到你所描述的那种感觉。对他们来说这显然是不可思议的，我还没想清楚怎么移植到我们这里，但这确实是非凡的，值得一看。我发现很多人就是无法——这里存在一个巨大的认知鸿沟。他们还没能接受这样一个事实：你可以随便提个要求，然后它很可能就做到了。我自己仍然会不够信任它能做到。大公司具体怎么做，我还不完全清楚。

确实感觉我们还缺少一层抽象——关于人类和 AI 如何在大规模场景下协作。小公司的优势在于基本就是 AI 在干活，不需要考虑跟很多人的协作接口。但我们会搞定的。
bilingual.en:
**Patrick Collison:** Um, Tempo is a new blockchain uh that Stripe incubated with Paradigm uh which obviously we're partners uh with uh with you guys on um and uh it the mainnet just launched but the project launched uh back uh last uh last summer. So it's a relatively new and uh and small team a couple dozen people. The Tempo team um set up a um a harness um a tool in their uh in their Slack installation for orchestrating pretty much everything at the company. Everything. You can just ask any task. Go and read these Google Docs. Uh turn those into a bunch of um uh a bunch of Linear tasks. Then go write a pull request to you know implement them. Uh then go deploy them and use our log analysis tools to test that the deployment actually worked and the agent will happily go and employ tool use across all of this and it's extremely trippy watching a whole organ — I mean a small organization but an organization of people do everything in a single Slack channel.

And I don't think that would scale to Stripe but it was the first time I had the experience you're describing which is it's clearly incredible for them I don't quite see how to transpose it for us but this is really something it's really something to watch. Um the and I find that a lot of people just can't — this is where there's a big overhang. They have not yet been able to wrap their heads around the fact that you can just kind of ask us anything and it'll probably happen. Um I I myself still find myself like not trusting quite enough that it's going to be possible. I don't know exactly how it's going to transpose to bigger companies.

It does feel like we're missing kind of like one more abstraction there um like how humans and AIs are going to interface at massive scale. The advantage that these smaller companies have is like it's just AIs. They don't have to figure out the interface with all the all the people. Um, but we'll figure it out.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 开源 AI，它的未来在哪里？
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Open source AI, where's it going? Does it have a future?

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 当然有未来。现在人们显然最想要的是更聪明、更快、更便宜的前沿智能，大部分需求都在那里。但开源也有很大的需求，我预计这个比例会随时间相对增加。
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** For sure. Uh, right now people clearly want smarter, faster, cheaper frontier intelligence and most of the demand is there. But there is also a lot of demand for open source and I expect that to increase relatively over time.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 我想聊一聊科学，因为我知道这是你非常兴奋的话题。而且这也跟我花了不少时间参与的 Arc Institute 有关。OpenAI 本身是一个基金会、一个非营利组织，最近向 Arc Institute 提供了一笔资助。也许我们一会儿再说细节，先请你聊聊 AI 应用于科学这件事，你观察到了什么，以及你对 OpenAI 基金会的资助策略的整体想法。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** So, I want to talk a little a little bit about science um because I know it's something you're very excited about. Um, but it's also uh relevant to uh to something I spend some of my time on uh which is uh which is the Arc Institute. Um and OpenAI is in fact a foundation um a nonprofit and recently um recently made a grant uh to the Arc Institute. Uh and so maybe we'll get to the details of that uh in a second but first you just want to speak a little bit to AI as applied to science you know what you're seeing and just how you think about grant making generally uh in the context of the uh OpenAI foundation.

bilingual.zh:
**Patrick Collison：** 关于 AI 和科学这个话题，我希望这将是 AI 这项技术对人类生活质量最重要的贡献。如果我们能够以更快的速度发现新科学——比如新材料、疾病治愈方法或其他任何东西——我相信，归根结底，生活之所以变得更好，是因为我们更好地理解了科学，然后用科学成果去建造东西、分发给人们。

从几个月前的模型开始，特别是现在有了 5.5，模型已经足够聪明，优秀的科学家会说"我能想出更好的研究思路了"，模型也能做出一些小但重要的发现。科学的步伐将会加速。最终我们会有自动化实验室、机器人去做各种事情，我们将能够更快地推进科学。如果我们能把过去需要十年才能完成的科学研究压缩到一年，这种复利效应以及我们能发现和做到的事情将是极其巨大的。

所以我认为这将是不可思议的。这也将是 OpenAI 基金会的重点关注领域之一——用资金、专业知识和技术来加速科学研究，并相信这些成果会以美好的方式惠及全世界。
bilingual.en:
**Patrick Collison:** So generally on on the AI and science question I I hope that this will be the most important contribution um of AI of this technology to human quality of life over — and that if we can start to discover new science at a much faster rate which can be like new materials or cures to diseases or any number of other things like I believe that to a first order approximation life gets better because we understand science better and then we figure out how to build stuff with it and distribute it to people.

Um starting with the models of a few months ago but really now with 5.5 the models have gotten smart enough that excellent scientists are saying I am able to figure out better ideas the models are able to make some small but important discoveries um and the pace of science is going to increase — eventually we'll have you know automated labs and robots and you know building who knows what uh and we'll be able to do science much faster. But if we can start doing like a decade of science of what it would have taken us in the old world in a year, the compounding effect there and what we'll be able to do and discover will will just be extremely great.

So uh I think this is going to be incredible and this will be one of the big areas of focus of the of the OpenAI foundation is uh basically like money and expertise and technology to accelerate science and trusting that that will you know flow to the world in wonderful ways.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 而且这将是一个规模很大的基金会。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** And this is going to be a big foundation.

bilingual.zh:
**Patrick Collison：** 是的，我认为它是最大的之一——甚至可能就是最大的——我相信它将成为世界上最大的基金会。我们重点关注科学，以及 AI 韧性（AI resilience）——帮助世界在拥有这项新技术的情况下顺利度过这个过渡期。

我们非常高兴能够支持 ARC。我认为它显然是 AI 和生物学交叉领域最优秀的研究机构。如果我们能通过这项技术、资本和基金会哪怕做出微小的贡献，帮助人们更健康、治疗疾病——整个"随着我们更好地理解生物学而能做到的事情"这一大类——我们会非常欣慰。我原以为这需要更长时间，但看到 Arc Institute 正在做的令人难以置信的工作，我现在觉得可能不会太遥远了。
bilingual.en:
**Patrick Collison:** Yeah, this will I think it is — it's one of the biggest maybe it's the biggest — I think it will be the biggest foundation um in the world. Uh and yeah, so we're really focused on science and then AI resilience um like helping the world through this transition uh with this new technology in it.

Um but you know, we were thrilled to get to support ARC. I think it's clearly the best sort of AI and bio effort and if we can make even a small contribution to with this technology and with the capital and the foundation to helping uh make people healthier, treat diseases, this whole cluster of what we can do as we get better at understanding biology. Um we will be very thrilled. I thought that was going to take longer and looking at the incredible work the Arc Foundation is doing, uh, I now think it maybe won't be won't be that far off.

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 在播客中间，通常会有一个插播广告环节。现在就是你的 Arc Institute 插播广告时间。楼下有一个 Arc Institute 的展位。你可能会好奇为什么一个互联网经济会议上会有它。

背景是这样的。Arc Institute 是我们四年前创立的一个机构。它的目标是实现人类历史上第一次对复杂疾病（complex disease）的治愈。复杂疾病是涉及遗传因素和环境因素共同作用的疾病。大多数癌症、大多数自身免疫疾病、大多数神经退行性疾病都属于这种特定意义上的复杂疾病。人类从未治愈过任何一种复杂疾病。一种都没有。我们治愈了很多传染病，我们知道如何筛查单基因疾病——就是由单一基因突变导致的那种。但我们从未治愈过一种复杂疾病。

所以我们创立这个机构就是以此为目标。Alzheimer's（阿尔茨海默病）是我们瞄准的第一个复杂疾病。我们的希望是，通过新的基因组编辑技术如 CRISPR 和 AI 的惊人进步，能够取得一些有意义的进展。机构只有四年历史，但早期成果非常令人鼓舞。

Arc Institute 目前正在寻找一位 CTO。之前有一位 CTO 在 ARC 做了一段 sabbatical（学术休假），他叫 Greg Brockman。他做了很棒的工作，帮我们训练了 Evo 2——这是有史以来最大的生物学基础模型。但我们正在找一位全职 CTO。我们想也许这个人就在今天的观众中，如果不是本人，也许他的朋友在。如果你知道可能感兴趣的人，去楼下看看 Arc 的展位吧。这就是你的插播广告。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** So, you know, in a podcast, um, midway through you might hear a little interstitial ad. Um, this is your interstitial ad for the Arc Institute. Um, there is, um, there's an Arc Institute booth downstairs. Uh and you might wonder why there's uh what what it's doing at the um at the internet economy conference.

Um and uh the uh the context here is so the the ARC Institute is an organization we started four years ago. Um and its goal is to uh produce hopefully the first cure for a complex disease in humans. So a complex disease is uh is one that involves some genetic factors and some environmental factors. So you can think of most cancers, most autoimmune disease, most neurodegenerative disease uh for example as being complex diseases in this kind of specific sense. And humanity has never cured a complex disease. Not one. We've cured lots of infectious diseases. We know how to screen for monogenic diseases for this one genetic mutation that um that undergirds it. We've never cured a complex condition.

Um so we started our institute with this is the goal. Alzheimer's uh is the first complex disease uh that we're uh that we're targeting. Um and our hope is that with both new genome engineering technologies like CRISPR and then sort of the amazing advances in AI uh that uh that we'll be able to make some hopefully meaningful progress and it's only four years old uh but the early results uh are very encouraging.

The ARC Institute is currently looking for a CTO. Um uh we had uh one CTO uh do a sabbatical at ARC uh last year uh his last year — year before. It was last year. Um and his name was Greg Brockman. Um and he did some great stuff. Uh he helped us train Evo 2, which is the largest biology foundation model uh ever trained. Um but we're looking for a full-time CTO. Um uh and uh the um uh we thought that well perhaps that person might be in this audience and if not their friend might be in this audience. So if you know somebody for whom that sounds of interest go check out the Arc stand downstairs and that's your interstitial ad.

bilingual.zh:
**Patrick Collison：** 我觉得你先声明这是插播广告，比直接开始宣传好多了。做得好。

好的，我们还没聊到 Stripe。你是 Stripe 的第二位投资人，对吧？
bilingual.en:
**Patrick Collison:** I think it's so much better that you labeled that as the interstitial ad rather than just doing it. That was good.

Um, okay. Well, we haven't talked about Stripe. So, um, you were the um, you were the I think it was second investor in Stripe.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** YC 是第一个吗？
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Was YC the first?

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 你和 Paul 是同时的。实际上是在同一个厨房里。所以也许你是第一个。我不记得支票是按什么顺序递出去的了。
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** You and Paul at the same time. Okay. In the same kitchen, in fact. Uh, so maybe you were first. In fact, I don't remember in which order the checks were handed over.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 对。那是 Paul 在 Palo Alto 的厨房。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Oh, yes. This was then his like Palo Alto kitchen.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 没错。当时我们就是两个长着青春痘的少年，提议要建一家金融服务机构。听起来很荒唐。你当时为什么投了？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Yeah, that's right. Um, so, you know, we were sort of, you know, we were two pimply teenagers. Uh proposing building this financial services institution. It sounded like a bit of a a ludicrous proposition. Why why did you invest?

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 说实话——他没有提前安排我说这些——你和 John 是我见过的最令人印象深刻的创始人之一。不论什么年龄段的，但尤其是在"青春痘少年"这个年龄段。我显然判断对了。

我之前就听说过你们，听 Paul 提起过你们，也在网上知道你们。让我印象深刻的是你们在解决自己遇到的问题。而且这个问题符合我认为将成为世界大趋势的方向。我当时非常相信电子商务将大规模转移到线上，会有大量创业公司涌现——这两点都暗示着 Stripe 可能会非常大。我一直信奉一个原则——现在也是——找到真正聪明的创始人加上一个将会很大的市场，投就对了，就这么简单。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** Um honestly, I I he didn't team me up for this. Uh you and John were two of the most — I I'd seen a lot of founders and you and John were two of the most impressive founders of any age, but certainly of pimply teenager age um that I had ever met. I clearly was right about that.

Um, and I had like known of you and I had heard Paul talk about you and I had like known of you on the internet. Um, and I was struck that you were solving a problem for yourself. Um, and that it was sort of like it fit a trend that I thought was going to be big in the world. Um like I kind of really believed that commerce was going to move online in a huge way and there were going to be lots of startups and both of those suggested that this would you know could be really big but um I don't know I was like a believer that if you can find really smart — and still am — if you can find like really smart founders and a market that's going to be big you should just invest and that's kind of it.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 根据你的整体视角，以及 OpenAI 使用 Stripe 的经验、你看到 OpenAI 当前和未来需要什么来构建业务——你对 Stripe 在 AI 时代的发展有什么建议？等一下，先确认一下我的记忆——你当初意识到自己需要支付功能，是因为你做了一个 iPhone 应用，可以下载整个 Wikipedia 供离线使用，因为你要去某个没有网络的地方。但收款很困难，所以——
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** And based on your um well just based on your general perspective in the world um but then also OpenAI's use of Stripe and what you've seen from that like what you see OpenAI needing and needing in the future and building the business and so forth. What's your advice for Stripe on navigating this um the AI era — before that just to check my memory the thing you were building the re the reason you realized you needed payments was you had like built this iPhone app to download all of Wikipedia because you were going somewhere crazy offline and it was like hard for you to take payments for it and that was like —

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 对，很困难。好的。我好久没想起这件事了。

我的建议更多是针对 Stripe 公司本身的。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** Yeah hard. Yeah. Okay. I hadn't thought about that in a long time.

Um I I think my my advice more towards like Stripe as a company itself.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 对。面向未来。
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Yeah. Going forward.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 现在是一个疯狂的时代，变化太多、太快。而且你是从客户的角度来看 Stripe 的，所以你有什么投诉和功能需求？
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Um like it's it's a crazy time in the world. Uh there's a lot changing a lot a lot happening. Um and again you've seen Stripe from the perspective of a customer. So what um you know what what are your complaints and feature requests?

bilingual.zh:
**Patrick Collison：** 其实更像是——既然我现在觉得我们应该更多地用类似 Stripe 这种模式来思考，我有一堆问题想问。在这方面我可能要向你学习的比你向我学习的更多。

因为我记得投资人以前总是自作聪明地说："Stripe 会成为大宗商品。公司做大了就会自己搭建支付系统。"结果是，虽然确实有多家支付服务商，但实际上，如果你做了一个好产品，人们未来还是需要收款的。如果你是一个靠谱的生态伙伴、不做疯狂的事，他们大概率会继续用你。我觉得这个逻辑会持续成立。

我确实认为每家公司都需要用 AI 提升效率，他们也会这么做。但这种"每家公司都会消失、一切都会完全不同"的心态——也许未来是 agent 之间需要互相处理支付而不是消费者和商家之间——但显然资金仍然需要以某种方式流动。我的建议是：拥抱 AI，特别是在内部使用 AI 来构建更好的产品，但不要假设整个社会经济体系会完全重组。我觉得现在世界在这方面有点太疯狂了。
bilingual.en:
**Patrick Collison:** Well, it's more like I want to — I now have — now that I'm thinking that we should be thinking more like a Stripe style model. I have a bunch of questions. I probably have more to learn from you than you you do from me here.

Um cuz I remember like when investors would outsmart themselves saying like, "Oh, Stripe's going to be a commodity. When everybody gets big, they're going to just like build their own thing." And it turns out that like yes, there are multiple payments providers. And yet in practice, if you make a great product, if you make like a great thing, people are still going to need to take money in the future. And they're probably just going to like stick with you if you're like a good reasonable ecosystem partner and don't do crazy things. Um, and I think that's going to keep working.

I I actually like I think every company clearly does need to get more efficient and with AI and they will do that. But this this whole mindset that like every company is going to go away and everything is going to be completely different and like maybe it'll be agents that need to handle payments between each other instead of, you know, consumers and merchants, but like clearly money is going to have to move somehow. And my my advice would be like adopt AI especially internally use AI to build better products but don't assume that the like entire socioeconomic system completely reconfigures. I think the world has gotten a little bit delusional about this.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 除了 AI 之外，展望未来十年，有哪些技术和领域让你兴奋？
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Apart from AI, as we look out over the next decade, just what are the technologies and areas that excite you?

bilingual.zh:
**Patrick Collison：** AI 本身在模型层和产品层当然让我兴奋。我现在对数据中心基础设施非常着迷——我觉得这里有太多很酷的事情可以做。我认为在物理层面会出现令人惊叹的新技术。能源、机器人——这一整个技术栈可能是我思考最多的。世界在脑机接口（brain machine interface）方面终于有了一些进展，这让我很兴奋。生物技术让我既兴奋又担忧。但我当然希望它能快速进步，而且防御性生物技术（defensive biotech）不幸地即将变得非常重要。

我对新型计算机交互界面也很兴奋。我觉得我们现在处在一个很荒谬的时期——我们困在老旧的设备和操作系统里，却拥有这项神奇的新赋能技术。Codex 很神奇，但让它去"使用我的电脑"——它在那里点来点去，操作那些为人类设计的界面，对 AI 来说根本不合理——我们可以做得比这好太多。我觉得还需要一套全新的互联网协议。所以，这些方向都让我很兴奋。
bilingual.en:
**Patrick Collison:** Um, sort of AI at the kind of model and product layer. Um I'm obsessed now with data center infrastructure — like relevant I think there's so much cool stuff to do there. Uh I think there will be amazing new technologies like at the physical layer. Uh energy, robots — those are made like that stack is probably what I think about the most. Um it does seem like the world is finally making a little progress on um brain machine interfaces. I'm excited about that. Uh I am excited about — well I'm both afraid of and I'm excited about progress in biotech. Um but you know I'm certainly hopeful that that can get much better quickly and and I think defensive biotech is about to become very important unfortunately.

Um I'm excited about new kinds of computer interfaces. I think we are in an insane area right now where we're stuck with these kind of old devices and old operating systems and we have this magic new enabling technology and it feels like Codex is amazing and what it — but it feels like very broken to be like telling this thing to use my computer and then it's like clicking around and there's all the stuff that was made for a person but doesn't really make sense for like an AI to go use — like we can do so much better there. I think there's a whole new internet protocol to make too. Um, so those things.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 你觉得世界上第一个盈利的核聚变反应堆什么时候会出现？
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** When do you think we'll have the world's first profitable nuclear fusion reactor?

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 取决于数据中心需求把电价推到多高。也许会比我们预想的更快。我猜在未来 5 年内。
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Depends how far electricity prices get pushed by data center demand. Maybe sooner than we thought. Um, I'll guess in the next 5 years.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 这是个大胆的预测。我希望如此。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** It's a bold prediction. I hope so.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 希望我们势头还在。超高音速商业航空呢？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Just going to hope we're on a roll here. Um, hypersonic commercial air travel.

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 我没太关注这个。超高音速是指 Mach 4 这种吧。估计要比较长的时间。我不确定——超过 10 年吧，也许稍短一些，大概这个量级。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Don't follow it as closely. Um, hypersonic meaning like Mach 4. Sure. A good chunk of time. I don't know — more than 10 years. Maybe a little less but something like that.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 好。有没有哪些科技领域目前还没有进入主流讨论，但你认为会被 AI 大幅加速，并产生广泛的社会影响？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Okay. Um are there any domains of science technology that are not in the discourse in a significant way that you think will be accelerated a lot by AI and will have broad kind of social consequence and impact?

bilingual.zh: **Sam Altman：** 有。我认为关注度严重不足的领域是材料科学（material science）。它不是一个很"酷"的方向。我觉得人们低估了这个世界有多少东西取决于材料，而 AI 在这个领域能取得多大进展——这是一个非常适合 AI 的问题。比如新型催化剂之类的方向。我预期那里会有非常快速的进展，会以非常积极的方式影响我们所有人的生活，但它得到的关注非常少。
bilingual.en: **Sam Altman:** Yeah. The one that I think just does not get enough attention is material science. Um, it's like a very — it's not a cool thing and I think people underestimate how much of the world is materials like how much of what we depend on and how much progress AI can make like it's such a beautifully AI-shaped problem — just getting new catalysts totally. Did I expect very rapid progress there and that it'll impact all of our lives in a very positive way and it gets like very little attention?

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** 最后一个问题。从某种意义上说，至少某种版本的 AI 是不可避免的。有很多人在朝着它冲刺，建造数据中心，推动一切前进。所以有一种宿命感。那你希望你个人的参与，相比于某种反事实假设，如何改变了世界的轨迹？
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Last question. Um it feels that in some sense at least some version of AI is kind of inevitable. There's lots of people rushing towards it and building it out and creating the data centers and uh and all the rest. So there's this kind of sense of determinacy. Um, how do you hope that your specific involvement changes the trajectory for the world relative to some other counterfactual?

bilingual.zh:
**Sam Altman：** 我信奉民主化、个人能动性和普及访问，我相信每个人都值得拥有真正美好的生活。OpenAI 历史上最具争议的决定就是我们现在所说的"迭代部署"（iterative deployment）。在我们发布 ChatGPT 的时候，当时很多人的想法是：这么做极其危险，你不能这样做。只有那一小群一直在思考 AI 安全的人才有资格知道即将发生什么。告诉世界就是一种"信息危害"（infohazard），太危险了，绝对不能发布。我们必须把它锁起来。在我们的象牙塔里，我们会发现这些美好的东西，我们会与世界分享成果，但 AI 掌握在我们手中，由我们控制。这种想法让我非常不舒服。

我当时就认为，而且现在仍然认为，避免这种权力集中极其重要。我们应该为世界而构建，让世界去使用它，用各种方式。有些用途会是好的，不是所有都会是好的。但通过让人们去探索摆在我们面前的这个广阔的机会空间——虽然过程中会有混乱，当然我们也会设置合理的安全护栏——我们给了世界一份礼物，而世界会在此基础上为所有人构建一份更大的礼物。如果你不让人们使用这项技术，如果你试图把它锁起来——现在这听起来可能很显而易见，但在我们出现之前，这确实是 AI 领域工作者的大致共识方案。我认为那样做会非常糟糕。

我是一个相信创业精神和创新的人，我相信人——人大多是善良的，大多会用工具做出了不起的事情。所以我认为我最大的贡献是、也将一直是——推动让 AI 成为一项民主化的技术，让人们能够使用它、在上面构建。
bilingual.en:
**Sam Altman:** Um, I believe in democratization and personal agency and access and that everybody deserves a really great life. Um, the most controversial decision we made in the history of OpenAI was um, what we now call iterative deployment. But a lot of the thinking at the time that we released ChatGPT was this was insanely dangerous uh to do. You can't do this. Only this like small set of people who have been thinking about AI safety can know what's coming. It's an infohazard to tell the world and it's too dangerous to ever release. We have to keep this locked up and we'll, you know, in our ivory tower, we will discover these wonderful things and we'll share the fruits with the world, but we'll have the AI and we'll control it. And that sat very poorly with me.

And I thought then and I believe now that it is extremely important that we avoid that kind of power concentration and that we build this for the world and the world gets to use it in lots of ways. Some of which will be good, not all of which will be good. Um but that the by enabling people to explore this very wide opportunity space in front of us, messy at times though it will be and obviously we'll put guard rails on it for reasonable safety — um we will give the world a gift, but the world will give — the world will build a much bigger gift on top of it for all of us. And that if you don't enable people with this technology and if you tried to keep it locked up, which again now this may sound obvious, but this like was the sort of rough consensus plan of people working on it before we came along. Um I think that would have been really bad.

I am a believer in entrepreneurship and innovation and and that people do — people are mostly good and mostly do amazing things with tools. Um so by uh I think my single biggest contribution uh has been will be whatever — pushing for this to be a democratized technology that people get to use and build on.

bilingual.zh: **Patrick Collison：** Sam Altman，非常感谢你。谢谢大家。
bilingual.en: **Patrick Collison:** Sam Altman, thank you very much. Thank you very much.
